coredata.dc:title: The phantom menace of dynamic soft-shorts in solid-state battery research
coredata.dc:creator: Counihan, Michael J.
Chavan, Kanchan S.
Barai, Pallab
Powers, Devon J.
Zhang, Yuepeng
Srinivasan, Venkat
Tepavcevic, Sanja
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state batteries with lithium metal anodes present the highest energy density batteries for applications in electric vehicles, leading to massive R&D investments over the past decade. Although most research focuses on preventing lithium metal dendrites that eventually short the battery, the nature of these shorts remains elusive. Soft-shorts, in particular, receive little attention or are not recognized, even in published data. Here, we present a comprehensive outline of the detection and analysis of soft-shorts in solid-state lithium metal cells with composite polymer electrolytes as well as a fundamental understanding of the dynamics of soft-shorts. Transient un-shorting of soft-shorts that occurs on the micro-to-millisecond timescale—driven by joule heating, chemical reactivity, and other processes—limits one’s ability to determine whether the cell was or is still shorted. We provide numerous experimental methods to detect and analyze soft-shorts in any battery type as a resource to all battery researchers.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cycling of symmetric cells

(A) Galvanostatic cycling of a 50 wt % LLZO in PEO-LiTFSI composite electrolyte in a Li||Li symmetric cell at 60°C. The current densities and plating times/areal capacities are noted for each set of cycles.
(B) Early lifetime voltage data show the expected profile with Ohmic and mass transport-related polarizations.

(C) Voltage profiles after high-current cycling show mainly Ohmic polarization with a small proportion of mass transport polarization, indicating that the cell resistance has decreased dramatically but is not hard-shorted.
Cell resistance analysis
(A) Select Nyquist plots from impedance performed during the cycling in Figure 1A.
(B) Inductance-corrected Nyquist plots over 25 cycles at 1 mA/cm2 cycling, showing a slight increase in impedance over time and low-frequency features.
(C) Comparison of the apparent DC resistance and the real component of the AC impedance at 1 Hz with cycling time of the cell.
(D) Cycling of the cell at 25°C after the cycling at 60°C in Figure 1.
(E) Ranges of apparent DC resistance from cycling data at the end of 60°C and 25°C cycling.
Simulated impedance response
(A) Equivalent circuit for a shorted cell, where the top elements in series represent the ionic processes and the lower elements in parallel represent the electronic pathways.
(B) Simulated Nyquist plots of a hypothetical cell with various Relec values.
(C) Apparent real impedance of the individual ionic elements with variable Relec.
(D) Qualitative comparison of a pristine cell (top) and soft-shorted cell (bottom) showing “compression” of the elements with lower frequency as electronic conductivity emerges.
Impedance analysis on cycled symmetric cells
(A) Nyquist plots of another 50 wt % LLZO composite Li||Li symmetric cell at various temperatures before (top) and after (bottom) the cycling protocol in Figure 1.
(B) Arrhenius-type plot of inverse real impedance at 100 kHz and 1 Hz before and after cycling.
(C) Impedance fits based on the equivalent circuit in Figure 3A without (top) and with (bottom) accounting for electronic conductivity in the circuit.

(D) Arrhenius-type plots of the fit resistances from (C). Error bars represent software-estimated error between the fit values and the experimental data for an individual measurement determined by the fitting software. The large error bars on some data points on the left plot also highlight the inaccuracy of the fit, since this model cannot resolve the low-impedance, medium-frequency features in the absence of the parallel electronic component.
Electronic current as a function of short size
(A) Electronic resistance as a function of filament diameter and length. The ranges of nominal cell resistance at room temperature for different electrolyte types are given in the boxes.

(B) Calculated fraction of the total current (0.1 mA/cm2 applied current density) carried by ionic and electronic pathways for a soft-shorted 100 μm-thick PEO-based polymer electrolyte cell at 60°C as a function of filament diameter.
Thermal stability of soft-shorts
(A) Simulated heat generation rate from a lithium filament in a soft-shorted cell with 0.1 mA/cm2 applied current density. Parameters are the same as in Figure 5B.

(B) Evolution of filament temperature over time from simulations with high Rionic accounting for moderate (h = 100 W m−2 K−1) and rapid (h = 103 W m−2 K−1) heat transfer between the filament and surrounding electrolyte.
(C) Heatmap relating filament size and heat transfer coefficient to the time for the filament to achieve a steady state or melting temperature (t ss).
(D) Effect of applied current density in a soft-shorted cell on filament thermal stability.
Transient nature of soft-shorts

(A) Galvanostatic cycling of a Cu||Li half cell with PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte at 60°C with 90 μA/cm2 applied current density. Points of interest in the plating and stripping steps are shown in (Ai) and (Aii).

(B) Schematic of the proposed un-short/re-short cycle of shorted filaments: (B1) joule heating, (B2) filament breaking due to melting or corrosion, (B3) replating of lithium on the now electrochemically active electrode, (B4) reconnection of the short with different morphology.
Soft-short behavior at open circuit

(A and B) Nyquist plots of two different 50 wt % LLZO composite Li||Li symmetric cells after cycling and soft-shorting, with impedance measurements made every 30 min in between open circuit rest periods.
(C) The quantified real impedance at 1 Hz as a function of time for the two cells.
(D) Schematic of the two proposed mechanisms for soft-short behavior at open circuit; (i) and (ii) correspond to the mechanisms responsible for the impedance changes labeled in (C).
Range of soft-short formation and behavior

(A) The formation of metal filaments in a symmetric cell or full battery can lead to electronic conductivity in parallel with ionic conductivity, with numerous factors influencing how much each pathway contributes to the overall cell current. Full electronic conductivity is considered a hard-short, whereas mixed electronic and ionic conductivity indicates soft-shorts.
(B) Short formation and mechanisms behind short dynamics can vary across all types of electrolytes but share some similarities.
Comprehensive list of methods for detecting and analyzing soft-shorts in symmetric, half, and full cells during and after cell operation

Voltage and current monitoring
voltage spike

a transient or permanent spike toward 0 V in the galvanostatic voltage profile likely indicates soft-short
easiest way to visualize as cell is running; short timescale for detection
instrument needs high enough voltage or time resolution settings to detect; spikes can result from system noise
Figures 1 and 7; this work
voltage profile
symmetric; half; full
the shape of the voltage profile deviates from expected Ohmic-plus-ionic shape
flat voltage and absence of curved profile from concentration polarization at higher currents/long cycle times indicate electronic current takes over

usually only shows at final stage of filament formation and shorting; soft-shorted cells can still have ionic-related curvature in profile even though most of current is electronic; single-ion conductors can show virtually flat profiles at low currents
Figure 1; this work
open circuit rest
symmetric; half; full
shorted cells should relax to 0.0 V rapidly in symmetric/half cells and self-discharge to lower voltages in full cells
voltage relaxes faster in soft-shorted cells than in fully ionic cells
small soft-shorts will not affect voltage response as much and will take long time to self-discharge; transient un-shorting over time can return cell to normal condition
Figure S5; this work
voltammetry
symmetric
slow cyclic voltammograms around 0 V are run to see if the current is stable/decreases (not shorted) or spies/increases with increasing cycles (soft-shorts forming)
totally linear V = IR response shows hard shorting

lacks control over current density; >1 h reported timescale for measurement; more valid for ceramic electrolytes where electronic conductivity is from grain boundaries; current deviations can reflect mass transport limitations or void formation, not necessarily soft-shorting
Wang et al. 15 and Gopal et al. 24

stripping in half cells or with thin lithium
symmetric; half
after desired cell cycling, all of the lithium is stripped from the metal current collector to compare charge stripped vs. charge passed
if the apparent stripping charge is higher than the expected plated lithium amount (i.e., Coulombic efficiency > 100%), then likely at least some of the current is electronic

since soft-shorts are redox-inactive but some amount of the anode is faradaically active, lithium can still be stripped at less than 100% efficiency but still leave shorts if Coulombic efficiency is inherently low and/or soft-short is small
Figure S4; Table S1; this work; Albertus et al., 19 Hill et al., 57 and Sahore et al. 58

full
the upper voltage limit is not reached in the amount of charge expected during charging step
inability to totally charge indicates loss of current to electronic pathway

excess charge below the voltage limit and voltage noise previously (but erroneously) attributed to electrolyte oxidation by cathode; transient un-shorting accelerated in full cells where cathode can chemically oxidize shorts

,
55 and Stolz et al. 56
Impedance
AC-DC resistance matching
symmetric; half; full
check that DC resistance (from R = V/Iapp) matches low-frequency real impedance
large discrepancies between these values or large changes over time indicate unusual cell behavior

if cell is shorted, then resistances will match anyway—no way to tell if fully ionic, mixed, or fully electronic current; small shorts that lower cell resistance by ≤20% might be attributed to electrode wetting/cell aging
Figure 2; this work; Zardalidis and Farmakis, 5 Wu et al., 9 and Günter et al. 40
inductive loop
symmetric half full
the appearance of an inductive loop at high frequency indicates the cell is shorted
if the impedance decreases drastically (usually <10 Ω cm2) with no features other than inductance and shunting to the real axis, then the cell is likely hard-shorted

inductance exists in every system, so the presence of a high-frequency loop does not necessarily mean the cell is shorted; well-engineered or high-area cells already have low impedance, harder to differentiate inductance and shunting due short from typical cell impedance
Cheng et al. 59
AC impedance fitting
symmetric; half; full
AC impedance measurements are performed at frequent intervals during the cycling protocol to check for electronic shunting
detailed analysis of all cell RC elements over time; quantification of ionic and electronic resistances

not every battery cycler equipped for impedance; quantitative analysis requires software and user knowledge; transient shorts (especially in full cells) may not show up when running impedance at the end of a cycle; harder to detect influence of smaller soft-shorts in low-resistance cells; small shorts that lower cell resistance might be attributed to electrode wetting/cell aging
Figures 2, 3, 4, and S7; this work; Li et al. 20
Temperature dependence
cycling at lower temperatures
symmetric; half; full
cycling at the same current density at lower temperatures shows if the cell is ionic (overvoltage increases with decreasing temperature) or electronic (vice versa)
smaller effective DC cell resistance with lower temperature indicates electronic pathway dominates cells
hours-long measurement problematic for transient soft-shorts; applied current risks un-shorting the cell; small soft-shorts accounting for <50% total current pathway not detected
Figure 2; this work; Li et al. 20
impedance analysis across temperature range
symmetric; half; full
AC impedance analysis as above but at various temperatures reveals electrochemical vs. electronic processes
quantifying activation energies (positive for ionic, negative for electronic) of each electrochemical and electronic process is the strongest way to detect soft-shorts
hours-long measurement problematic for transient soft-shorts; same as above “AC impedance fitting” disadvantages
Figures 4 and S7; this work
Imaging and chemical detection
optical microscopy
post-mortem; operando
deposited metal morphology is imaged at the millimeter to micron scale
filament morphologies easily shown to penetrate the electrolyte under given cycling conditions; can match visual growth stage exactly with short appearance in time

usually imaging from the side; need transparent or very thin electrolytes like liquids or well-made ceramics and polymers to image clearly; optical cells with long inter-electrode distances do not replicate real cell conditions, can influence cell behavior and metal growth morphologies

Bai et al., 23 Zhu et al., 60 Geng et al., 61 and Chae et al. 62
,
63
scanning electron microscopy
post-mortem operando
deposited metal morphology is imaged at the micron scale
possible to image top of current collector or interior during metal plating/stripping; useful for solid electrolyte cross-section analysis
electron-sensitive materials can spontaneously plate lithium; lack of elemental lithium signature hard to detect filaments vs. cracks
Krauskopf et al., 16 Fuchs et al., 64 and Peng et al. 65
transmission electron microscopy
post-mortem; in situ
deposited metal morphology is imaged at the nanometer scale
nanometer resolution probes true soft-short dimensions; nanoscale in situ experiments with solid electrolytes; electron energy loss spectroscopy and electron diffraction can identify lithium metal
careful air-free or cryogenic sample preparation; beam-sensitive samples will plate Li or degrade Li at standard temperatures; finding “needle in a haystack” for post-mortem soft-short detection
Peng et al., 65 Zhai et al., 66 Diaz and Kushima, 67 Singh et al., 68 and Wang et al. 69

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The transient nature of soft-shorts presents a challenge for all detection and analysis methods; methods that are more affected by spontaneous un-shorting of soft-shorts are noted in the “disadvantages” column.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: A shared disadvantage for “imaging and chemical detection” materials characterization techniques is the detection of lithium growths/dendrites at large, not necessarily a specific soft-short(s).

head.ce:title: The phantom menace of dynamic soft-shorts in solid-state battery research

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Division, Argonne National Laboratory, Lemont, IL 60439, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state batteries with lithium metal anodes present the highest energy density batteries for applications in electric vehicles, leading to massive R&D investments over the past decade. Although most research focuses on preventing lithium metal dendrites that eventually short the battery, the nature of these shorts remains elusive. Soft-shorts, in particular, receive little attention or are not recognized, even in published data. Here, we present a comprehensive outline of the detection and analysis of soft-shorts in solid-state lithium metal cells with composite polymer electrolytes as well as a fundamental understanding of the dynamics of soft-shorts. Transient un-shorting of soft-shorts that occurs on the micro-to-millisecond timescale—driven by joule heating, chemical reactivity, and other processes—limits one’s ability to determine whether the cell was or is still shorted. We provide numerous experimental methods to detect and analyze soft-shorts in any battery type as a resource to all battery researchers.

Solid-state batteries using lithium metal anodes are one of the hottest topics in research today, offering the highest energy density for electrical vehicles and other applications. Many studies highlight their prevention of dendrites that cause the cell to fail by short-circuiting through the electrolyte. However, the nature of these shorts is extremely variable, and some works still fail to address the appearance of soft-shorts in their data. Soft-shorts result in the simultaneous flow of ionic and electronic current, so both symmetric and full cells can appear healthy when, in reality, the cell has already failed. Here, we provide and highlight numerous complementary techniques for identifying and analyzing soft-shorts in all types of batteries, using lithium metal batteries with composite polymer electrolytes as an example system. This work serves as a reference to all battery researchers for presenting accurate data free of soft-shorts to the field moving forward.

Short-circuiting is the worst-case scenario for lithium metal batteries. However, soft-shorts are small, highly variable, and transient short-circuits that can lead to misguided data interpretation and precede permanent battery failure. This work presents numerous characterizations of soft-shorts in solid-state batteries along with modeling of soft-short dynamics. A comprehensive guide to analyzing soft-shorts in all battery and electrolyte types is presented as a reference for the field.

ce:section.ce:para:
High energy density all solid-state batteries with lithium metal anodes have been described as the “holy grail” of battery technologies. 1
,
2 The number one priority of material and cell design in this space is preventing the uncontrolled growth of lithium during charging, resulting in lithium growths that penetrate the electrolyte—the infamous dendrite problem. 3
,
4 If the plated lithium contacts the cathode, the cell can internally experience an electrical short circuit, with the metal growth responsible for this being called a short. Shorts can lead to lower Coulombic efficiency from internal self-discharge in the best case 5
,
6 and thermal runaway leading to catastrophic battery failure in the worst case. 7
,
8 Although most research focuses on preventing dendrites—and thus shorts—in the first place, the conditions under which shorts form vary widely across different electrolytes and cell conditions. 9 Even batteries with non-metal anodes, like graphite-based lithium-ion batteries, are prone to metal plating during overcharging 10
,
11
,
12
,
13 or local heating, 14 making metal plating-caused shorting an issue for all battery types with metal ion electrolytes. The extent to which shorts form, the impact on the electrochemical cell response, and short behavior over time are therefore understudied and underappreciated, despite shorts (not dendrites) being the direct cause of cell malfunction.

Shorts can be defined as either hard or soft. Hard-shorts with low electronic resistance permanently short the cell, prevent charging to a non-zero voltage, and offer the clearest indication of cell failure. On the other hand, soft-shorts—known alternatively as “micro short circuits,” 5 “soft breakdown,” 15 “dynamic short-circuits,” 16
,
17 “internal short circuits,” 18 or undifferentiated from hard-shorts at all—are more elusive in both definition and identification. As such, the lack of knowledge in the literature about shorts has led to erroneous data being published with the promise of high capacity and charging rates despite the cell being soft-shorted. 19 Some recent works have focused on providing electroanalytical tools to identify shorts in solid sulfide electrolytes 15 and aqueous zinc metal batteries, 20 although every tool has its limitations and may not be applicable to different battery systems.

Here, we present the most comprehensive study to date on the detection and analysis of soft-shorts in lithium metal batteries, with particular attention paid to the dynamics of soft-shorts that make them difficult to identify and analyze. Solid composite polymer electrolytes (CPEs) were tested under high-current density to create conditions where soft-shorts form. Surface-level analysis of galvanostatic voltage profiles and alternating current (AC) impedance cannot conclusively determine if the cell is or was soft-shorted. Only a detailed, temperature-dependent quantification of ionic and electronic circuit elements from impedance data identifies that a cell is soft-shorted, with significant amounts of both ionic and electronic current passing through the cell simultaneously. Modeling of soft-shorts as a thin lithium filament offers insights into the observed transient nature of soft-shorts formed through dendrite growth. A complex interplay of joule heating from electronic current carried through the filament, heat transfer from the lithium to the electrolyte, the applied current density, and ionic resistance in the cell all determine whether the filament will reach a stable temperature or burn itself out by melting. This leads to a continuous un-shorting/re-shorting cycle within the cell that forms multiple soft-shorts and can stabilize to a “permanently soft-shorted” cell that is not necessarily hard-shorted. Simulation and experiments show that most soft-shorts form and burn out on the micro- to millisecond timescale, making identification of soft-shorts over hours, days, and months of cycling a challenge. Finally, we offer a comprehensive list of electrochemical and materials characterization techniques to identify soft-shorts in any electrolyte and battery system. The advantages and limitations of all approaches creates the need for judicious application of multiple approaches to confirm a healthy battery without soft-shorts.

To begin, it is helpful to define a “soft-short” and what differentiates it from a “hard-short.” We will define a hard-shorted cell as a cell with permanent electronic connection between the anode and cathode within the cell that renders it unable to be charged or pass usable ionic current. The cell voltage goes to zero, and the effective resistance of the cell with electronic short is usually under 10 Ω cm2. We will use area-specific resistances in units of Ω cm2 to refer to global cell resistances (which may or may not include electronic resistance from a short) and nominal resistances in units of Ω to refer to specific short resistances when appropriate.

By “softening” these hard-short characteristics, then, a soft-short is an impermanent electrical connection between the two electrodes that allows both electronic and ionic current to flow in the cell, which will not necessarily have zero voltage at short times and may have effective resistance above 10 Ω cm2. The amount of current flowing through electronic paths vs. ionic paths will be dependent on the dimensions of the shorts, the ionic resistance of the electrolyte components, and other physical characteristics, as will be shown below.

In lithium metal anode research, “short” is often synonymous with “dendrites” since a dendrite is the fastest way plated lithium can grow between electrodes and lead to a short. However, there are other ways shorts can develop—electronic conductivity in the electrolyte, 21
,
22 unwanted contact between current collectors in the cell, 18 or non-dendritic growth. This last path is an important differentiation to make from dendritic growth, as there are conditions at which “mossy,” “filament,” or other plated metal morphologies grow but dendrites (determined by the Sand’s time or limiting current where the electrolyte salt concentration in the plating electrode goes to zero) do not. 23
,
24
,
25
,
26
,
27 Any lithium growth into the electrolyte can lead to shorting, not just dendrites. In this work, we will use the terminology short to describe a connection between anode and cathode that changes the electrochemical response, “filament” to refer to lithium growths of unknown shape within the experimental cells or describe specific lithium growths treated in our models, and “dendrite” only when appropriate in a specific context.

CPEs with 50 wt % Al-doped Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO) nanofiber fillers in a poly(ethylene oxide) and lithium bis(trifluorosulfonylimide) (PEO-LiTFSI) matrix were fabricated as described in the experimental procedures section. 28 Relative to the rest of the solid-state electrolyte literature, CPEs that incorporate inorganic fillers in a polymer matrix are a lesser-studied electrolyte type but are beneficial to studying the general behavior of soft-shorts for the following reasons that will become clear later in this work. First, unlike other solid electrolytes like ceramics and inorganic glasses, electronic conductivity through the bulk electrolyte or grain boundaries is extremely low and does not contribute to electrical shorting, 29
,
30
,
31
,
32 so it can be neglected in the equivalent circuit modeling. Second, CPEs have multiple ways to spontaneously un-short themselves, and one goal of this work is to identify as many dynamics related to soft-shorts as possible. One pathway unique to composites is the razor effect shown by Mezzomo 33 and our group, 28 where the filler physically breaks a lithium filament or at least deflects its pathway to the counter electrode. CPEs with 50 wt % (20 vol %) LLZO nanofibers exaggerate this to the largest extent while still having usable ionic conductivity at 25°C–60°C, which is the reason for the specific ratio used here. Third, removing the fillers to yield a pure polymer electrolyte isolates the un-shorting effects due to filament joule heating and corrosion with no contribution from the razor effect. In these ways, CPEs present a useful electrolyte system for studying the dynamics of soft-shorts.

To push the critical current density limits of the composite electrolyte, samples in symmetric Li||Li cells were cycled for 30 min between 0.1 and 1.0 mA/cm2, then 1–8 mA/cm2 at 1 mAh/cm2, then 1 mA/cm2 at 1 mAh/cm2 for several days (Figure 1 A). Smooth voltage profiles are observed at 0.1 mA/cm2, and mostly smooth at 0.25 mA/cm2. The voltage profiles show an initial Ohmic polarization followed by sloping behavior indicative of ionic mass transport (Figure 1B), consistent with an un-shorted cell where current is 100% ionic. At 0.5 mA/cm2 and above, several voltage spikes are observed, along with a general decrease in voltage-to-current density ratio relative to the 0.1 mA/cm2, i.e., the apparent R in V = IR has decreased. This is even more pronounced in the 1–8 mA/cm2 testing, where the voltage of 8 mA/cm2 is 2.2 times lower than that at 1 mA/cm2. Clearly, the functional resistance of the cell is decreased significantly from the as-prepared state, but notably, it reaches a steady state after 75 h and even increases very slightly at longer times.

To the trained electrochemist, this is almost definitely a soft-shorted cell. But how can the generator and viewers of the data be totally sure? Some may attribute the decrease in resistance to some sort of “conditioning” of the cell or wetting of the electrode interfaces to increase surface area that lowers total resistance. There are several quick checks common among researchers to confirm soft-shorting. First, sudden voltage spikes toward zero usually indicate soft-shorting as filaments touch the other electrode (or possibly meet in the middle of the electrolyte in the case of Li||Li symmetric cells). In this case, there are spikes downward for sure but also spikes upward. Bidirectional spikes can indicate system noise (from the potentiostat, temperature fluctuations, etc.), but it would be in bad faith to assume all of these spikes are related solely to electrical noise. These particular data were also acquired at short sample times (every 5–10 s), and long-term cycling may only sample every minute or more and not necessarily capture these voltage spikes. Second, hard-shorted cells show flat voltage profiles in galvanostatic cycling, something termed the “fake stable” phenomenon by Lu et al. 31 After nearly 300 h of cycling, these cells still show Ohmic and mass transport-related polarizations, albeit at vastly different magnitudes and ratios compared with the initial cycling (Figure 1C).

Apart from the spikes, the total resistance dropping by almost two orders of magnitude is a clear sign of soft-shorting. But what if the drop is only 10% or 20%, as is common in many cell tests reported in the literature? What if only the data from 75 to 250 h were shown, and the cycling up to that point was never mentioned in this publication? Qualitative inspection of the voltage profile alone is not enough to conclusively identify or quantify soft-shorting. Thus, we turn to electrochemical impedance analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simple soft-short checks based on impedance
ce:section.ce:para: Impedance measurements were performed every five cycles in the cycling data in Figure 1. Select Nyquist plots are shown in Figure 2 A. The as-prepared cell has high impedance (>1,700 Ω cm2 at 1 Hz) typical of unoptimized solid electrolyte interfaces, with readily quantifiable bulk, interface, charge transfer (CT), and Warburg impedances (Figure S1). After the first 25 h of cycling, the impedance drops to below 400 Ω cm2 but maintains the same shape as the original plot. After 8 mA/cm2, drastic changes are seen with the real impedance around 30 Ω cm2 and only two semicircles easily resolved. Clearly, something drastic took place during cycling, and the cell is likely soft-shorted. However, more cycling at 1 mA/cm2 leads to an increase in impedance over time (Figure 2B), and a small capacitive tail attributable to ionic diffusion is seen at low frequencies. Typically, a hard-shorted cell would not change impedance over time, since no electrochemistry is taking place, and would shunt to (i.e., completely touch and end at) the real axis in Nyquist plots. Does the appearance of these two features imply the cell is not shorted?

Another common check for cell shorting is comparison of the direct current (DC) resistance from galvanostatic cycling and low-frequency AC impedance. In theory, these values should be near identical in a normally functioning cell: RDC = Vobserved/Iapplied ∼ Zreal at ≤1 Hz. Figure 2C compares the median DC resistance (from the galvanostatic voltage profiles, RDC = Vmedian/Iapplied) of each cycle and the real impedance at 1 Hz. In the beginning, RDC is much higher than Zreal due to the arbitrary choice of frequency for impedance analysis—this is expected since Zreal will only equal RDC at very low frequencies or low resistances. After 25 h, in line with the most dramatic voltage spikes and voltage decreases in the cycling profile, this difference decreases, but RDC/Zreal is still greater than unity. This does not confirm or disprove the theory of cell shorting—even if the cell is (hard-)shorted, it should hold that RDC/Zreal = 1. Thus, the only time this check is informative is if RDC < Zreal, which would clearly indicate unusual cell behavior, as if the cell is shorted during cycling but un-shorted during the impedance measurement.

Li et al. 20 proposed a method for confirming soft-shorts in zinc metal batteries, which display similar shorting phenomena and impedance characteristics as those observed here. A soft-shorted cell controlled mainly by electronic conductivity should show lower RDC in the cycling profile as the temperature is decreased since metal resistivity decreases with decreasing temperature. Cycling of this Li||Li cell at 25°C was performed after shorting at 60°C (Figure 2D), and the effective RDC was quantified (Figure 2E). The resistances are not statistically different at 60°C or 25°C—this is unexpected for a fully ionic cell but also does not meet the criteria for a “harder” soft-short based on Li’s criteria. 20 One notable difference that indicates electronic current accounts for most of the cell current is the almost completely flat, Ohmic-only voltage profiles in Figure 2D. Relative to the initial voltage profiles at 60°C dominated by mass transport, this indicates clear soft-shorting where electronic conductivity dominates at lower temperatures. However, even this check is not applicable to all systems, especially in the field of solid electrolytes. Electrolytes with single-ion conducting properties (i.e., the Li+ transference number is near or equal to 1) such as garnets, 34 sulfides, 35 and single-ion conducting polymers 36 show very flat voltage profiles at low and moderate current densities. Thus, for a weakly soft-shorted single-ion conductor, none of the above analyses could prove the existence of soft-shorts. A better method is needed to make soft-short identification applicable to all electrolyte and battery systems under any condition.

To provide this general and accurate method, we turn to in-depth, quantitative impedance analysis to both prove the existence of soft-shorts and quantify the extent of soft-shorting, first in theory and then in practice. Typical equivalent circuits for solid electrolytes involve series combinations of parallel resistor and capacitive elements assigned to bulk (and grain boundary) ionic transport, interfacial (i.e., solid electrolyte interphase [SEI]) resistance, CT resistance, and diffusion. 37
,
38 These are fully ionic and can be thought of as “faradaic” processes, in that charge flowing through this part of the circuit involves transport of ions to and from the interface and some electrochemical reaction like lithium plating and stripping. In a soft-shorted system, these processes are in parallel with an electronic, “non-faradaic” pathway, which can be modeled as a resistor and capacitor in parallel. The capacitor is included due to presence of two larger current collectors in the cell, but this capacitance is generally very low (<10−9 F) 39 and can often be neglected. 15 The effective equivalent circuit is shown in Figure 3 A, with capacitive components and Warburg impedance in the faradaic branch treated as constant phase elements.

To demonstrate the effect of soft-short formation on the impedance response, Nyquist plots were simulated with sample values typical of solid electrolyte batteries (Figure S2) for a total impedance around 250 Ω cm2. The resistance of the electronic pathway was varied over several orders of magnitude, with 106 Ω representing virtually no electronic conductivity and 100 Ω representing a hard-short. The Nyquist plots in Figure 3B show that as Relec decreases, the apparent real impedance decreases as well, with low-frequency elements diminishing in their contribution to the overall response. Once Relec is low enough, it shunts the impedance spectrum down to the real z axis near what the actual Relec value is. This is quantified in Figure 3C, showing that diffusional impedance (Warburg) is the first to disappear, followed by CT and interface (Int) elements, then finally bulk impedance. This also shows that until Relec is within two orders of magnitude of the resistance of a given element, there will be no evidence in the impedance spectrum at these frequencies (107 to 10−1 Hz are shown here). Figure 3D shows qualitatively how the emergence of electronic conductivity “compresses” the Nyquist plot. This effect is also apparent in Bode plots (Figures S2C–S2F), where the phase angle trends toward 0° with decreasing Relec and away from the theoretical −45° phase of the Warburg element at low frequency.

It is clear that increasing electronic conductivity lowers impedance. But as mentioned briefly above, there may be other real, physical changes in the cell that lower impedance from the as-prepared state, e.g., wetting of electrode interfaces and temperature equilibration that lower Int and CT impedances at early times in the cell lifetime. 5
,
9
,
40 To truly dissect the impedance response and prove that a short exists, impedance spectra were collected at different temperatures. Variable temperature is important because ionic conductivity, SEI conductivity, and CT rate should increase with increasing temperature, whereas electronic conductivity through lithium metal should decrease with increasing temperature. 41 When plotted in Arrhenius-type plots common in the literature—some form of inverse resistance vs. inverse temperature—the ionic elements will have a positive Arrhenius activation energy (negative slope), and the electronic element will have a negative activation energy (positive slope). This is related to the method proposed by Li et al. 20 demonstrated in Figures 2D and 2E. The caveat with that method is that soft-shorts will only manifest if the short accounts for more than 50% of the current flow at the tested temperatures; if ionic current is still the majority pathway, then the cell polarization should still increase with decreasing temperature. Conversely, quantitative impedance analysis and Arrhenius relationships can, in theory, detect soft-shorts at any amount of shorting detectable within the error of the measurement and given a temperature range where the electronic shunting manifests.

Figure 4 A shows the impedance spectra of another 50 wt % LLZO composite electrolyte Li||Li cell before and after the same cycling protocol shown in Figure 1. With increasing temperature, the total impedance of the cell decreases in both cases, though much more dramatically before cycling. Figure 4B shows an Arrhenius-type plot for inverse real impedance at high and low frequency. Before cycling, the trends and activation energies are consistent with a fully ionic, pristine cell. After cycling, the slopes are still positive at high and low frequency, though with much lower apparent activation energies (Ea = 0.02 and 0.12 eV after cycling compared with Ea = 0.82 and 0.81 eV before cycling). Also of note is that in the cycled case, the resistances at high and low frequency converge to similar values at lower temperature, whereas the pristine case shows the low-frequency impedance consistently separate from that at high frequency. This heavily implies soft-shorting, since it is unlikely that any ionic process (bulk transport, SEI transport, etc.) has an activation energy as low as 0.02 eV. Still, separation of the individual components will shed light on soft-shorting.

Since the impedance does increase with lower temperature in the soft-shorted cell, impedance analysis that does not account for electronic conductivity could still erroneously fit the impedance spectra to bulk, interface, and CT processes. Figure 4C shows two scenarios of impedance fitting without and with the electronic pathway in the equivalent circuit (Figure 3A). In both cases, the fits are good (χ2 ≤ 1.5 × 10−5 for all fits). How do we know which is more representative of the real cell? Figure 4D shows an Arrhenius-type plot of inverse resistance of the fitted individual elements. In both cases, bulk resistance has a positive activation energy, agreeing with real ionic processes. With no electronic component in the model, interface resistances show a positive activation energy from 40°C to 60°C, although the very low value (<0.01 eV) indicates some problem, much like the post-cycling case in Figure 4B. However, interface and CT resistances have negative activation energies overall when not accounting for electronic conductivity. This is incongruent with any common electrochemical process in batteries, and only when electronic resistance is included do these processes have the correct Arrhenius relationships, i.e., positive activation energies.

The electronic resistance used in the fits is also plotted in Figure 4D with an apparent slope Ea = −0.02 eV. Due to the number of circuit elements to fit, and the uncertainty of the soft-short magnitude, two assumptions were made to provide consistent fits. The electronic resistance was set at each temperature point by assuming (1) a full electronic shunt occurs at the lowest tested temperature, here 50.2 Ω at 25°C, and (2) adjusting the Relec at each temperature based on the known resistivity of lithium metal with increasing temperature, which yields an apparent Ea around −0.02 eV in this temperature range. 41 Since the temperature dependence of lithium metal is the most known quantity in this system, we chose to fix the Relec values for each fit. Since the actual filament structure and number of shorts are unknown, and such assumptions must be made, this analytical treatment is semi-quantitative with respect to determining the actual resistance of the short. If Relec can be determined with certainty (i.e., the electrical shunt is very obvious at lower temperatures), then this may become truly quantitative with respect to the collection of all shorts represented as Relec in the circuit as well as the other elements. Quantifying the resistance of a single short is outside the realm of possibility with this method unless a single short can be proven with imaging methods.

Although fixing the electronic component may not provide the most accurate values for the other elements’ resistances, those values are close enough to the true values to establish qualitative trends, which are the main indicators of soft-shorts with this method. As such, this fitting procedure provides the correct Arrhenius relationships to definitively prove soft-shorting more than any other method to date. There are still some drawbacks with this method, due to the dynamics of soft-shorts discussed shortly. First, the un-shorting of cells during the multi-hour measurement is an issue and again limits the true quantification of shorts. Since un-shorting can be driven by corrosion at high temperatures, we recommend only decreasing the temperature with each new impedance measurement, then performing a final measurement at the original temperature to ensure the cell has approximately the same impedance as before to prove the cell did not un-short during the protocol. Second, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a specific tool not available to every researcher or on every battery cycling instrument. It may be possible to use galvanostatic voltage profiles to separate some of the ionic process resistances, as described recently by Stolz et al. 42 Again, though, the application of current can greatly impact soft-shorts through dynamic processes, as shown below, so using impedance methods where perturbations around open circuit (potentiostatic EIS) or low currents (galvanostatic EIS) might still be preferable.

Temperature-dependent impedance analysis can prove and quantify cell shorting, from hard-shorts down to small soft-shorts. This approach should always be valid as long as the short does not increase or decrease in resistance over the timescale of the measurements. In reality, we have observed that many soft-shorts in CPEs are transient, both during cycling and resting at open circuit. The instability of shorts in lithium metal batteries has been observed for almost two decades, something known as the “fuse effect” where some shorts will burn themselves out. 43 Even continued low-current cycling has been shown to remove (at least partially) soft-shorts in LLZO solid electrolytes. 44 This is important to be aware of, since analysis after cycling or much later post-mortem analysis on used cells may reveal an un-shorted cell, even though it was shorted at some point during its lifetime. Here, we detail some of the physical and chemical processes that lead to dynamic shorting, un-shorting, and re-shorting, regardless of electrolyte type. 31

In polymer and composite electrolytes as well as liquid electrolytes with polymer separators, all electronic conductivity will be carried through metal growths in the cell. The thickness and length of a prototypical lithium filament will control the electrical resistance following:

For simplicity in the following arguments, we will assume that the resistivity of a nanometer-scale lithium filament is the same as bulk lithium metal near room temperature (10−7 Ω m) at all temperatures up to the melting point, 41 filament length is the same as the cell length (i.e., electrolyte thickness), and the filament is cylindrical with constant area throughout the length of the filament, such that

The electrical resistance in a single filament is plotted as a function of filament diameter in Figure 5 A at various lengths. The approximate number of lithium atoms in the filament cross-section is estimated given a cubic structure with 3.51 Å lattice parameter. 45 Overlaid are approximate lab-scale cell resistances for typical liquid and solid-state batteries. This shows that soft-shorts in solid polymer cells will generally be more apparent than in liquid cells. For example, a 10 nm-thick filament will contribute significantly to the impedance response in a 25 μm polymer electrolyte, whereas a filament would need to be around 40 nm thick to have the same effect in cells with liquid organic electrolytes.

The most distinguishing feature of soft-shorts is the flow of ionic and electronic current in parallel. Since current will flow through paths of least resistance, and filament resistance is inversely proportional to diameter, larger filaments will account for more of the total current. We built a model using simple electronic circuit arguments, much like that of Wang et al., 15 to determine the relative amount of current as the filament dimensions change. For a galvanostatic measurement, the effective cell voltage at steady state will be determined by the applied current:

Figure 5B plots the fraction of current flowing through ionic and electronic paths in a cell similar to the experiments in Figures 1, 2, and 4: a 100 μm cell length and 1 cm2 area at 60°C under 0.1 mA/cm2 applied current density. The condition of “high Rionic” uses the 1,170 Ω cm2 resistance observed for the unoptimized composite electrolytes, whereas “low Rionic” uses the 34 Ω cm2 bulk resistance only and presents a scenario where interface and CT impedance are negligible (i.e., through cell engineering) for a more practical battery comparison. As expected, higher ionic resistance results in larger electronic current for a given filament diameter. Relating this to the experimentally observed soft-shorts in Figure 4 at 60°C and applying Equations 3, 4, 5, and 6, we estimate that 24% of the current is ionic and 76% electronic, which would correspond to an effective filament diameter of ∼300 nm if the short is due to a single lithium growth. However, there are assuredly many smaller filaments or branched structures that formed during the multiple short/un-short spikes during galvanostatic cycling, which cumulatively contribute to the low electronic resistance—more details are discussed below. These parallel paths can still be analyzed as a single resistance in impedance modeling, leading to the same quantified values for current distribution (Figure S3).

It is also important to realize that soft-shorts, like any metal passing electronic current, will experience joule heating. This has been proposed as a mechanism for self-healing of lithium dendrites, 46 and is equally applicable to healing soft-shorts, dendritic or not. The short will heat during cell operation, with the rate of heat generated equal to:
(Equation 7)

The heat generation rate during steady-state galvanostatic operation for the same simulation as Figure 5B is plotted in Figure 6 A. Interestingly, a maximum in the heat generation rate is seen for both low and high Rionic conditions. For small filaments, the resistance is so large that little current flows through the short. For large filaments, the resistance is so low that Q is small. For intermediate filaments 100–1,000 nm in diameter, enough current can flow through the resistive short that large quantities of heat are generated. These quantities are greater for cells with higher ionic resistance since more current flows through the short.

Generated heat will build up in the lithium metal but also transfer to the surrounding electrolyte medium, leading to some equilibrium temperature. This heat transfer was incorporated into the model via the following relationship:

Where m is the mass of the filament determined by the length and diameter, c
p is the heat capacity of lithium metal (taken as 3.6 J g−1 K−1 over the whole temperature range), dT/dt is the change in temperature of the filament over time, h is the heat transfer coefficient between the lithium and surrounding medium, A
surf is the surface area of the filament exposed to electrolyte, and T
electrolyte is the baseline temperature of the electrolyte around the filament. Note that the increase in electrolyte temperature due to joule heating and/or heat flow from the lithium filament is not modeled in the present context, and the electrolyte temperature is assumed to be same as the baseline operating temperature since the heat generation from the filament affects a minute volume compared with the larger cell. The temperature increase over time for 1–1,000 nm filaments is plotted with moderate (h = 1 W m−2 K−1) and high (h = 1,000 W m−2 K−1) heat transfer coefficients in Figure 6B. If the heat transfer is not rapid enough, all filaments will melt, although at vastly different timescales. Most filaments are predicted to melt before 100 μs with applied current, but larger filaments can survive up to 100 ms before reaching 180°C, the melting point of lithium. If heat transfer is rapid, then any filament can stabilize to temperatures below this threshold, and the steady-state temperature will depend on the filament size controlling the joule heating, mass, and surface area terms in Equation 8.

This model predicts the fuse effect, where electronic shorts will spontaneously burn out, as shown in polymer electrolytes over several hours 43 and distinctly obvious in Figure 1A. One revelation is the vast timescales over which this effect can manifest. Figure 6C provides a 3D map of the time for the filament to reach a stable temperature or the melting point. Small filaments achieve this limit on the micro- to millisecond timescale, whereas thicker filaments can take seconds to hours. The heat transfer coefficient is the main determinant as to whether the filament will melt or remain stable. Since joule heating scales with the square of current through the filament, larger currents should accelerate melting. Figure 6D shows the melting/stability limits of the same cell conditions with 0.01, 0.1, and 1 mA/cm2 applied current density. The expected rise in this limit is seen, where the filament will melt unless heat transfer is extremely rapid at higher current densities. Heat transfer coefficients between steel and polymers have been measured in the 103–104 W m−2 K−1 range, 47 so one would assume that most filaments should be stable. However, due to the reactivity of lithium metal, a native SEI always exists on the surface. This complicates the heat transfer process in two ways. First, the accumulation of inorganic surface layers is known to lower the effective heat transfer coefficient in polymer systems. 48 Second, the spatial, temporal, and chemical heterogeneity of the nanometer-scale SEI 49
,
50 complicates the heat transfer process, both outward through the lithium-SEI-electrolyte interface and along the filament, possibly leading to thermal gradients known to influence lithium filament behavior. 51 Thus, the effective heat transfer coefficient in this lithium-polymer electrolyte system is probably nearing the threshold at which all but the largest filaments will melt.

Joule heating in a shorted cell presents one pathway for transient soft-short behavior. If the current passed through an individual short results in joule heating that is not compensated by heat loss to the surrounding electrolyte, the short can “burn itself out,” instantly lowering the total electronic conductivity and increasing the amount of ionic current in the cell as a result. This burn-out may proceed through two mechanisms. First, the increased temperature at the lithium metal surface will increase reactivity with the electrolyte, reacting with SEI components or fresh electrolyte to chemically corrode the short. Second, the lithium metal may reach temperatures where it melts (∼180°C), leading to a drastic change in mechanical properties that cause the lithium to disintegrate.

All of these processes create a complicated physical and chemical space where it appears unlikely that a soft-short passing electronic current can survive intact for any long period of time. We investigated this transient short nature with galvanostatic cycling of PEO-LiTFSI solid polymer electrolytes with extremely small (1 ms) sampling intervals. A solid polymer electrolyte was used without ceramic nanofillers due to possibility of the filler breaking the shorts through some physical process (e.g., the so-called “razor effect”) 28
,
33 aside from joule heating. A Cu||Li half-cell configuration was also used since the un-shorted cell voltage should be non-zero when fully stripped, whereas a shorted cell will go to 0.0 V. This cell format also allows the detection of soft-shorts by monitoring Coulombic efficiency and noting deviations from the expected value (Figure S4; Table S1). At 90 μA/cm2 applied cell current density, soft-shorts should rapidly heat and possibly melt, leading to the sharp voltage increases seen in Figure 7 A. Interestingly, these un-shorts are frequently followed by re-shorts, a phenomenon that can be observed any time metal plating occurs, such as stripping in half cells where lithium re-plates at the counter electrode. In Figure 7Ai, a soft-short degrades over a 5 ms period in the plating step, followed immediately by re-shorting that stabilizes the voltage in 1–3 ms. This re-short results in a cell voltage even closer to 0 V than the previous shorted state, indicating the reformed short and/or other shorts formed in that time are larger in size after this degradation-(re)formation process.

The reverse process is seen in Figure 7Aii, where short forms rapidly in 1 ms with a 7 ms gentle decrease to a steady-state voltage, followed 8 s later by un-shorting. This un-shorting exhibits a gradual increase in cell voltage across 70 ms, followed by a rapid increase to the un-shorted cell voltage over 7 ms. This could indicate the very slow heating and then quick melting of a single short or a more complicated process involving many shorts. If multiple shorts exist in parallel, the electronic current will be distributed through them according to their resistances (i.e., size). If one filament heats to the point of un-shorting, then the other shorts may experience an increased current load, depending on the fraction of current taken over by the ionic pathways. This can lead to further heating, melting, and un-shorting of the shorts—a cascade effect that, if strong enough, could un-short the entire cell. This is indeed the case in Figure 7A, where the cell briefly returns to its expected voltage of 0.08 V, indicating that only ionic processes are taking place at that point.

The un-short/re-short process is depicted in Figure 7B, where a small gap from a heated and broken short could pass faradaic current between the positively and negatively charged electrodes. This lithium plating regrows the short, or even multiple shorts, causing re-shorting of the cell and joule heating. If this process continues many times over the course of the cell life (a loop of steps 2–4 in the figure), it is expected to converge to a thermally stable state where enough shorts have formed to distribute low enough electronic currents through a “forest” of soft-shorts, causing the cell to appear permanently shorted while also displaying ionic and electronic character. This is believed to be the mechanism responsible for the stable voltage profiles and impedance of the cells in Figures 1, 2, and 4. A comparative simulation of a single 100 nm-diameter filament and 100 parallel 10-nm filaments is shown in Figure S5, where the effective filament area and thus electronic resistance are the same. However, due to the lower individual filament currents and higher surface area of the parallel shorts, the smaller filaments barely increase in temperature, whereas the single large filament approaches the melting point of lithium within 1 ms. This result helps confirm the existence of permanent soft-shorts, and not hard-shorts, in these electrolytes.

This un-short/re-short phenomenon has been known to occur at larger length scales without lithium metal plating. Zhang et al. 18 demonstrated that joule heating and melting can effectively un-short contact between aluminum and copper current collectors in a shorted lithium-ion battery, but the resulting high electric field in the melted micro-gap is hypothesized to lead to a large discharge event that re-shorts the cell. It is possible this “fusing phenomenon” also occurs in the case of nanometer lithium filaments here but with much more regular re-shorting events.

Finally, we investigated the stability of soft-shorts at open circuit. In theory, when no current is flowing and the shorts experience no joule heating, the soft-short should persist. However, chemical reactivity of lithium metal with the electrolyte is inevitable, especially at elevated temperatures (most cells tested in this work were at 60°C). Additionally, in composite electrolytes with hard ceramic fillers, the Brownian motion of the fillers can break the filaments, thereby un-shorting the cell physically through the razor effect. 28
,
33

Two more 50 wt % LLZO composite electrolytes in lithium symmetric cells are shown in Figures 8 A and 8B. After cycling to soft-shorting at current densities below 1 mA/cm2, the cells were allowed to rest at open circuit, with impedance measurements performed every 30 min. For cell A (Figures 8A and 8C), there is a gradual increase in the cell impedance for several hours before an order of magnitude increase within a 2-h window. The cell reaches impedances greater than that of the as-prepared cell due to prolonged SEI growth, indicating complete removal of soft-shorts and return to 100% ionic processes. This ≥100% of the un-shorted cell impedance threshold is important to claim that the cell is completely un-shorted. Previous works have demonstrated a partial increase of impedance from the soft-shorted state under certain cycling or resting conditions, but failure to completely recover the initial state makes claims of total un-shorting dubious or at least uncontrollable in light of our demonstrated impedance analysis. 44
,
52 Although the time for a cell to un-short at open circuit can vary from minutes to days or even longer, we have found that over 90% of the (composite) polymer electrolyte cells tested under these conditions un-short themselves to a fully ionic state, whether in symmetric, half-, or full-cell configurations.

However, in some instances, further shorting of the cell at open circuit was detected. As seen in Figures 8B and 8C, the impedance of cell B remains steady after galvanostatic cycling for 2 h before dropping from 91 to 43 Ω cm2. This unexpected result indicates a growth in the effective short size, leading to lower electronic resistance. A possible explanation is the further growth of lithium deposits and shorting during the incidental plating and stripping during the impedance measurements. However, in this case, there is a shift in the open circuit potential profile in the middle of the rest stage and no shift during the impedance measurement (Figures 8B and S6). We have also observed this spontaneous shorting during the open circuit rest less than 1 min after plating lithium in PEO-LiTFSI half cells (Figure S6). The faradaic current passing through the cell during the open circuit measurement is expected to be less than 1 fA, given the 1 TΩ input impedance of the potentiostat, assuredly not enough to contribute a 2-fold increase in electronic conductivity. Until further experiments can probe this phenomenon, we tentatively attribute this spontaneous increase in electronic conductivity to short fusion with other shorts or dead lithium. If one filament contacts another shorted filament, the effective resistivity of the soft-short decreases as the electronic current is spread over a larger area. Based on our simple filament model (Equation 1), a drop from 91 to 43 Ω cm2 would be equivalent to a 50% increase in filament diameter across the length of the cell if the filament grew along its diameter. Assuming a more realistic scenario like Figure 8D where a dead lithium filament contacts an existing short to make a branched path over 80% of the cell length, then the dead lithium filament would need to be ∼70% larger than the existing short. Given how much dead lithium can be generated in these composite electrolytes, 28 and that dead lithium is known to be a byproduct of shorts un-shorting through heating, 53 these scenarios are not out of the question. In the end, these fused shorts are prone to the same degradation over time as any lithium filament in these composite electrolytes, resulting in gradual and sharp increases in cell impedance over many hours (Figure 8C). However, this indicates that although cells will eventually un-short, this is a stochastic process that cannot be relied on from a cell analysis or safety perspective. These observations speak to the varied and elusive nature of soft-shorts and the challenge of characterizing them in situ and ex situ.

We have demonstrated the detection, analysis, and transient dynamics of soft-shorts in symmetric and half cells with lithium metal and solid polymer and composite electrolytes. How well do the lessons learned here apply to other battery types with different electrode and electrolyte chemistries?

For full cells with a cathode of any type, the impedance analysis can still apply, and transient voltage spikes during galvanostatic charging are still good indicators of soft-shorts. The added layer of complexity is the multi-faceted voltage profile and how the chemistry of the cathode un-shorts the cell more than in symmetric cells. When a full cell cannot completely charge to the voltage limit in the expected capacity range, it was previously attributed in literature to oxidation of the electrolyte causing excess charge loss. However, recent works showed that this behavior can be ascribed to soft-shorts between the anode and cathode, even though the cell voltage is far from 0 V. 6
,
54
,
55
,
56 We observe that un-shorting phenomena are more exaggerated in full cells than symmetric cells (Figure S7) since the lithium filament can react chemically with the cathode (e.g., Li0 + FePO4 → LiFePO4 and 2 Li0 + LiFePO4 → Fe0 + Li3PO4) in addition to joule heating, which prolongs the onset of permanent soft-shorts. This continued chemical reaction between cathode the soft-short leads to quick un-shorting even at open circuit in a heavily shorted cell (Figures S7C and S7D), which limits the applicability of the post-cycling impedance protocol used above in symmetric cells (Figure S7F).

For non-polymer and non-lithium solid-state batteries, similar analyses and transient observations have been reported. In ceramic sulfide electrolytes, grain boundary electronic conductivity and electrolyte chemistry are responsible for growing soft-shorts. 15 This is shared by numerous solid ceramic and glass electrolytes, making electronic conductivity an important parameter to control. 22 Molten sodium batteries also experience soft-shorting phenomena, despite the absence of solid metal deposits that lead to dendrites typically thought of with lithium solid-state batteries. Hill et al. recently provided visual evidence of the parallel ionic and electronic pathways in molten sodium symmetric cells with a solid ceramic NaSICON separator. 57 Sodium metal buildup in the ceramic was attributed to spontaneous Na reduction within the separator due to non-zero electronic conductivity in addition to the typical current focusing leading to dendrites. Transient spikes in the voltage profile, lower impedance with increased cycle time, and only 66% stripping of the Na metal determined by mass for a theoretical full charge highlight that soft-shorts leading to parallel ionic and electronic current can manifest under a wide range of experimental conditions for numerous reasons.

For liquid and gel electrolytes with lithium metal, voltage spikes and transient behavior are expected and seen, much like the case of solid polymer electrolytes in all cell formats. 9 This is due to the similarity of joule heating, heat loss, SEI formation, and chemical reactivity processes described above with polymer electrolytes. Even with non-metal anodes like graphite, overcharging leading to lithium filament formation presents a case where soft-short analysis may need to be applied. 10
,
11
,
12
,
13 Impedance and temperature-dependent analysis has also been applied to aqueous liquid electrolytes with zinc metal anodes. 20 We have seen that soft-shorts in zinc metal batteries also present dynamic un-shorting and re-shorting behavior, as shown in Figure S8. It is noteworthy that soft-shorts in this system seem more persistent than lithium metal, which we attribute to lower corrosion rates and better heat loss to the surrounding aqueous electrolyte during filament joule heating. However, although slow, the corrosion of zinc in neutral-to-acidic electrolyte will occur eventually, leading to un-shorting of the cell given enough time under applied current or at open circuit. Thus, it is important to consider transient short behavior in all electrolytes and cell designs. Different materials properties and cycling conditions can lead to differences in soft-short formation and behavior (Figure 9 ), but these shorts can nonetheless be characterized by similar analytical tools.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implications for research and practical applications
ce:section.ce:para: Soft-shorts are diagnosable but also transient, and not every technique works for a given battery system. Soft-shorts could be anything from a forest of lithium growths accounting for >95% of the current flow to a thin dendrite in a crack or grain boundary that only decreases the cell impedance by 10%. How does the field reconcile promising cell performance with this reality? Re-posing the question at the beginning of this work: How does one look at a set of data and know if the cell is soft-shorted or not?

First, it takes critical analysis and honesty on the part of the data author to report full datasets, replicates of cells and electrolyte conditions, and as full as possible supplemental analysis apart from galvanostatic cycling (i.e., impedance and temperature studies where appropriate). One would hope this to be true in every published work, yet some neglect obvious signs of soft-shorting. 19 It is difficult for any reader to judge whether or not a cell is soft-shorted when the authors provide little other data or zoomed-in plots of certain figures. This duty of identifying faulty data and requesting more information also falls on reviewers—and, as a last resort, readers—and we hope this work and others 15
,
20 will be useful references for all. To this end, Table 1 lists 18 electrochemical and materials characterization techniques to detect, analyze, and visualize soft-shorts in lab-scale cells, along with their advantages and disadvantages. These fall into five general categories: voltage and current monitoring, charge efficiency, impedance, temperature dependence, and imaging and chemical detection. Short circuit analyses at the large cell pack level are also an area of development, using algorithms to analyze state-of-charge characteristics and determine if the cells (soft-)shorted. 79
,
80 The main disadvantage across all techniques is how difficult it is to capture the soft-short when detected—the sub-second transient nature of soft-shorts makes post-mortem analyses especially challenging.

Second, there is a larger question of whether soft-shorts matter at all. Hard-shorts are the true “battery killers” and possible safety hazard. Does it matter if a few nanometer-scale filaments form and burn out in a matter of microseconds, losing a small amount of dead lithium but one that is overall negligible? On one hand, there is a zero-tolerance policy for any form of dendrite formation and shorting, soft or hard. This is especially applicable to long-term applications like electric vehicles, where battery lifetime and safety are some of the most important factors. On the other hand, for smaller, low-current, or low-cost applications, perhaps there is less concern. For batteries and devices that are self-healing, easily replaceable, and pose no direct safety hazard, there might be more tolerance for batteries even if they experience soft-short failure mechanisms. One such application would be microbatteries in low-current sensors for “the internet of things.” 81
,
82 Due to cost reasons, this would be not applicable to lithium metal batteries, but cheaper aqueous zinc metal batteries would fall into this category of low-cost, easily recyclable batteries for remote or non-vehicle applications.

Overall, our view is that soft-shorts should be treated as what they are: the first step off the cliff toward battery failure. If an electrolyte soft-shorts, it can and will hard-short when given enough time and current. However, the analysis of soft-shorting—particularly how electrolyte properties influence the formation and transient properties of shorts—provides crucial insight into material design. Entire cells and electrolytes should not be thrown out wholesale because they soft-shorted; they should be given a critical eye and used as lessons learned to streamline better battery research.

This work probes the dynamic behavior nature of soft-shorts in lithium metal batteries with solid polymer and ceramic composite electrolytes. We show clear evidence of soft-shorting in CPEs with 50 wt % LLZO nanofiber ceramic filler through drastic changes in the galvanostatic cycling and electrochemical impedance profiles over the cell lifetime. However, soft-shorting cannot be conclusively identified when analyzing a limited part of the dataset. Semi-quantitative impedance analysis demonstrates that soft-shorted cells can still present acceptable-looking Nyquist plots and pass simple soft-short checks, and only by separating the temperature-dependent electrochemical processes (bulk transport, interface, and CT resistances) from an electronic resistance incorporated in the fitting model can soft-shorts be unequivocally diagnosed. Computational models incorporating ionic conductivity of the electrolyte, soft-short resistance as a function of filament size, joule heating of the soft-short under applied current, and heat transfer between the short and electrolyte were developed. These accurately describe the timescale at which soft-shorts un-short under applied current, typically tens of micro- to single milliseconds, due to melting and/or accelerated corrosion during joule heating. The ability of soft-shorts to form, un-short, and re-short under applied current, as well as degrade and possibly fuse together at open circuit, is demonstrated. Finally, a comprehensive list of techniques for analyzing soft-shorts is provided. This work outlines the elusive nature of soft-shorts and provides tools for battery researchers to address soft-shorts in all battery types.
Further information and requests for resources and materials should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Michael J. Counihan (mcounihan@anl.gov).
This study did not generate new unique materials.
Data and code used to generate the figures in this study are available as supplemental information.

We fabricated composite electrolytes based on PEO-LiTFSI and Al-doped LLZO nanofibers. A ceramic fraction of 50 wt % LLZO was used to maximize the mechanical effects of the ceramic filler while still having usable ionic conductivity (2 × 10−4 S/cm at 60°C). These were fabricated via roll-to-roll electrospinning and annealing of the LLZO nanofibers, followed by solvent mixing and coating to yield the final freestanding composite membrane, as described previously. 28 Briefly, a homogeneous mixture of PEO (600 kDa) and LiTFSI in a 15:1 EO:Li mol:mol ratio were mixed in acetonitrile. For composite electrolytes, Al-doped LLZO nanofibers fabricated from electrospinning and annealing processes were also added to the mixture at the reported weight percentage. These slurries were coated via slot-die onto a moving web and allowed to dry in ambient atmosphere. Electrolytes were then punched into 5/8″ diameter pieces and vacuum transferred to the glovebox for cell assembly. All chemicals for these processes were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

Samples were tested in 2,032 coin cells with two 0.5 mm stainless steel spacers and a wave spring. Lithium metal foils (250 μm thick, MTI Corporation) were polished to yield a pristine surface before being punched and assembled in the cell. Electrodes typically had geometric areas of 1.0 cm2. Working electrodes were Li foil for symmetric cells or Cu foil (6 μm thick, MSE Supplies) for half cells, with Li foil counter electrodes in all cases. Full cells used lithium iron phosphate (LFP, 12 mg/cm2, MTI Corporation) with binder coated on Al foil as cathode and Cu foil as anode; the LFP composite cathode was impregnated with a small amount of PEO-LiTFSI solution and dried prior to cell assembly. After cell assembly at room temperature, cells were slowly ramped up to operating temperature, typically 60°C. Electrochemical measurements were acquired with a Biologic VSP-300 potentiostat/galvanostat. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was performed with a DC potential of 0.0 V vs. open circuit and 10 mV AC amplitude in the frequency range of 7 MHz to 1 or 0.1 Hz. Impedance analysis was carried out with ZView software (Scribner) using various equivalent circuits to fit the system, as described in the text. Galvanostatic cycling was performed at the reported current densities and charge capacities.
